issue: Fulfillment of Article VI disarmament obligations

entries.position_summary: The country welcomes broad support for risk reduction measures and asserts that such measures complement Article VI disarmament objectives, contributing to the prevention of nuclear war. It frames risk reduction as aligned with the NPT’s disarmament commitments.
entries.quote: Second, we would like to welcome the progress made on key issues. For example, we welcome the strong support of many States Parties for risk reduction. As we have frequently stated, risk reduction complements Article VI goals and contributes to our common interest in preventing nuclear war.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its Article VI obligation to pursue good-faith negotiations on nuclear disarmament and commits to doing so through bilateral treaties, multilateral fora, and practical actions to advance NPT disarmament goals.
entries.quote: The United States stands by our obligation under the NPT to pursue negotiations in good faith on effective measures on nuclear disarmament -- in bilateral treaties, through multilateral fora, and actions to advance NPT disarmament goals.

entries.position_summary: The country supports negotiating an FMCT and urges China to join other NPT nuclear-weapon states in adopting a moratorium on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons pending such a treaty, framing this as part of fulfilling Article VI obligations.
entries.quote: Article VI. Pending such a treaty, we hope the PRC will join the other NPT nuclear- weapon States in adopting a moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights it has declassified information on its nuclear stockpile and argues that greater transparency of nuclear arsenals supports nonproliferation and disarmament, including fulfilling NPT commitments. It frames transparency as a concrete step toward meeting treaty obligations.
entries.quote: And it is why the United States just released newly declassified information on our nuclear weapons stockpile. We believe that increasing the transparency of states’ nuclear stockpiles is important to nonproliferation and disarmament efforts, including commitments under this treaty.

entries.position_summary: Supports regular reporting by States parties on implementing the 2010 Action Plan and Article VI, and encourages nuclear-weapon States to adopt standardized reporting formats and agreed intervals within the strengthened review process.
entries.quote: 7. States parties have long reported to the Review Conference on their actions in support of the Non-Proliferation Treaty. As was stated in the consensus action plan from the 2010 Review Conference, “States parties should submit regular reports, within the framework of the strengthened review process for the Treaty, on the implementation of the present action plan, as well as of article VI”, along with disarmament actions recommended by the 1995 and 2000 Review Conferences (action 20). The action plan also encouraged the nuclear-weapon States to agree on a standard reporting form and to determine appropriate reporting intervals (action 21).

entries.position_summary: The United States supports negotiating an FMCT, has engaged in UNGA FMCT initiatives, and maintains a unilateral moratorium on producing fissile material for weapons while urging China to follow suit. It cites irreversible disarmament steps—dismantling plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions via arms control with Russia, and fissile material disposition—and endorses the principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. The U.S. advances verification capabilities through the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE.
entries.quote: We will also engage constructively in the other subsidiary bodies, including on the Cessation of the nuclear arms race and nuclear disarmament. In addition to our efforts at the CD, the United States actively participated in two UN General Assembly FMCT initiatives in 2014-2015 and 2017-2018 that we believe laid important groundwork for future FMCT negotiations. We continue to maintain our unilateral moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and urge the PRC – the only Nuclear Weapon State that has not done the same – to join us. 2000 Steps Five and Thirteen; 2010 Actions Two and Eighteen: Irreversibility and Further Development of Verification Capabilities: On Steps Five and Thirteen, the United States has undertaken important steps to support the principal of irreversibility, including through irreversible dismantlement of all weapons-grade plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions through arms control treaties with Russia, disposition of fissile material from nuclear weapons, and other efforts. We also support language from the 2022 RevCon Draft Final document that acknowledges the mutually reinforcing principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. Active U.S. engagement in the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE underscore our commitment to engaging a broad set of countries on the development of future tools for arms control that support irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency in nuclear disarmament. We also welcome the group statement on irreversibility in nuclear disarmament delivered by Norway,

entries.position_summary: The United States argues against adopting benchmarks, targets, and timelines for nuclear disarmament within the NPT review process, asserting they overlook worsening security conditions, national security needs, practical and political constraints, and risks of non-compliance. It views such proposals as not improving the review process.
entries.quote: However, the United States also recognizes there are some proposals that would not improve the review process. For instance, proposals that seek to establish benchmarks, targets, and timelines for nuclear disarmament may appear beneficial, but fail to recognize the worsening security environment, national security concerns, practical challenges, political realities, and the risk of non-compliance.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores regular national reporting to NPT meetings, including its Nuclear Posture Review, as evidence of a strong commitment to transparency and progress on disarmament. It supports enhancing reporting and interactive discussion to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process and will incorporate state feedback to expand future reports.
entries.quote: The United States submitted national reports to the 2014 NPT Preparatory Committee, an update for the 2015 NPT Review Conference, an update including on actions over the previous six years for the 2022 Review Conference, and an update to that report providing a link to our 2022 Nuclear Posture Review. Before the practice of “national reports,” the United States submitted similar information to previous Review Conferences. Collectively, these demonstrate the steady U.S. commitment to transparency and making real progress on disarmament. We welcome constructive efforts to improve NPT reporting and strongly support calls to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process through interactive discussion of national reports. As we approach future U.S. reports, we will consider feedback from interested States regarding specific transparency elements they would find helpful. Based on that feedback, we will seek to address additional topics in our future national reports where possible.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its commitment to disarmament consistent with the Thirteen Steps and Article VI, stating readiness to pursue critical arms control measures. It seeks to reduce nuclear risks through bilateral engagement with Russia and China and via the P5 process, despite their current unwillingness to engage.
entries.quote: The “Thirteen Steps” clearly identified the need for all Nuclear Weapon States to engage in the disarmament process. It is because of the seriousness with which we take our disarmament-related commitments that President Biden made clear at the UN last September that “no matter what else is happening in the world, the United States is ready to pursue critical arms control measures.” It's also why we will continue to seek to reduce nuclear risks with Russia and the PRC bilaterally and through the P5 process, despite their unwillingness to engage.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency through its NPR and stockpile disclosures, reports steep unilateral reductions including in non-strategic weapons, and highlights measures lowering operational status and the role of nuclear weapons in its strategy. It encourages China and Russia to adopt similar transparency. This is presented as part of fulfilling disarmament commitments and advancing risk reduction.
entries.quote: well as in the public release of information in our Nuclear Posture Review and pertaining to our nuclear weapons stockpile. The U.S. national report to the 2022 RevCon also provided detailed information on other relevant topics, including steep U.S. reductions in non-strategic nuclear weapons and other unilateral cuts beyond treaty-based reductions over the past three decades, significant measures taken to reduce operational status of nuclear weapons systems, and the reduced role of nuclear weapons in U.S. national security strategy, including with respect to the 2022 NPR’s Declaratory Policy. We encourage the PRC and Russia to provide similar transparency.

entries.position_summary: The statement credits the NPT with containing proliferation, establishing robust IAEA safeguards, and advancing disarmament through stockpile reductions and arms control. It also praises the spread of nuclear-weapon-free zones and the benefits delivered by peaceful nuclear applications.
entries.quote: Since it entered into force, we have contained nuclear proliferation; developed robust international safeguards to monitor peaceful nuclear programs; made significant steps towards disarmament through stockpile reductions and other arms control measures; seen entire regions adopt nuclear-weapon-free zones; and, through the peaceful uses of nuclear energy, science, and technology, improved the lives of millions.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms full commitment to the NPT and to implementing Article VI, viewing the NPT as the sole effective framework for achieving nuclear disarmament. It pledges to continue implementing relevant past disarmament commitments from the 1995, 2000, and 2010 NPT consensus documents.
entries.quote: Crosscutting Priorities from 2000 “Thirteen Steps” and 2010 Action One: The United States remains fully committed to the provisions and purposes of the NPT and has taken significant strides to implement article VI. We continue to view the NPT, in contrast to other instruments, as the only viable, effective framework for addressing the enormously complex military, political and technical requirements for the elimination of nuclear weapons. We will continue to pursue implementation of our past disarmament-related commitments, where they remain relevant, including those identified in the 1995, 2000, and 2010 consensus documents.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China for rapidly expanding its nuclear arsenal while avoiding engagement, and questions whether China is committed to nuclear restraint and meeting its Article VI disarmament obligations. It implies concern that China's behavior undermines progress toward disarmament.
entries.quote: • And the People’s Republic of China is rapidly expanding its nuclear arsenal while avoiding engagement. Given the circumstance, it is reasonable to ask whether the PRC genuinely shares a commitment to nuclear restraint and the NPT’s Article VI goals.

entries.position_summary: The delegation emphasizes implementing Article VI and prior Review Conference commitments. It supports interactive consideration of reports and outcome‑oriented PrepCom discussions to agree recommendations that enhance the review process’s effectiveness, efficiency, coordination, continuity, transparency, and accountability.
entries.quote: NPT/CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 implementation of article VI and commitments undertaken in final outcomes of review conferences. The consideration of the reports should provide for an interactive exchange. The working group recommends that the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference should continue to engage in outcome-oriented discussions, with a view to agreeing on recommendations on improving the effectiveness, efficiency, coordination, continuity, transparency and accountability of the review process, on the basis of a balanced consideration of proposals presented during the working group.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms strong support for the NPT and Article VI, highlighting an 88% reduction of its nuclear arsenal, dismantlement of thousands of warheads, and elimination of entire classes of delivery systems. It also notes a moratorium of over 30 years on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons.
entries.quote: The United States remains a strong supporter of the NPT, including the disarmament-related obligations contained in Article VI, and we have taken significant steps in this area, reducing U.S. nuclear arsenal by 88 percent, dismantling thousands of warheads, and eliminating entire classes of nuclear delivery systems. For more than 30 years, we have implemented a moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons or other nuclear explosive devices. These are effective measures that 1

entries.position_summary: The country reports an 88% reduction of its nuclear stockpile and seeks to further lessen the role of nuclear weapons in its defense strategy, while acknowledging current security conditions. This frames its actions as contributing to disarmament and provides a degree of transparency.
entries.quote: We have reduced the number of nuclear weapons in our stockpile by over 88% and, while fully cognizant of the current security environment, continue to seek opportunities to reduce the role of nuclear weapons in our national defense strategy.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its unequivocal commitment to a world without nuclear weapons and to pursuing general and complete disarmament under effective international controls. It underscores the value of bilateral arms control, notes past reductions, and expresses readiness to engage Russia and China on future frameworks and risk reduction, while warning that Russia’s noncompliance jeopardizes prospects for a post-2026 New START successor. It aligns with the P5 view that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought.
entries.quote: the substance of which we support. 2000 Steps Six and Eleven; 2010 Actions Three and Five: Unequivocal Undertaking to Eliminate Nuclear Arsenals and Reaffirmation of Objective for General and Complete Disarmament: On Steps Six and Eleven, the unequivocal U.S. commitment to the peace and security of a world without nuclear weapons cannot be doubted, and consistent with the 2022 P5 Joint Leaders statement, we recognize that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought. The only way to totally eliminate all nuclear risk is through complete, irreversible, global nuclear disarmament. And we remain committed to the Thirteen Step’s call for pursuing in good faith measures toward general and complete disarmament under strict and effective international controls. 2000 Step Seven; 2010 Action Four: START II, START III, and ABM Treaties: On Step Seven, the 2000 Review Conference spoke to the importance of bilateral arms control agreements. Over the past three decades, the United States has made significant reductions in nuclear weapons – both unilaterally and through arms control agreements with the former Soviet Union and then Russia. The arms control environment has clearly evolved since 2000, and specific topics from this step have been overtaken by developments. We have made clear our readiness to engage on future arms control frameworks with Russia and to work to reduce nuclear risks with the PRC. However, Russia’s noncompliance with existing obligations and outright rejection of arms control dialogue casts a shadow over the likelihood of a New START successor after February 2026. Meanwhile, the

entries.position_summary: The United States (with France and the UK) emphasizes transparency and responsible practices, declaring excess fissile material and placing it and all civilian nuclear facilities under IAEA safeguards while annually reporting civil plutonium holdings. It frames these steps as risk-reduction measures that prevent miscalculation and support progress on disarmament commitments under the NPT. The statement contrasts this approach with what it describes as the opacity of China and Russia.
entries.quote: On Steps Eight and Ten, the United States has also declared hundreds of tons of fissile material in excess to nuclear weapons needs and has made much of it available for IAEA safeguards during storage and disposition. In stark contrast to the PRC, the United States makes all our civilian nuclear facilities available for IAEA safeguards and reports annually to the IAEA our civil plutonium holdings. 2000 Steps Nine and Twelve; 2010 Actions Nineteen through Twenty-One: Other Steps for NWS, Transparency, and Reporting: On Steps Nine and Twelve, the P3 commitment to transparency is striking in comparison to the opacity shown by the PRC and Russia. The Working Paper submitted to the 2022 RevCon by the United States, France, and the UK on “Principles and responsible practices for Nuclear Weapon States” represents our shared commitment to transparency and to acting responsibly to reduce nuclear risks, prevent unintended miscalculation and escalation, and support progress on our disarmament commitments. This commitment is also reflected in our NPT national report, which lays out in greater detail the actions I am describing, as

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms commitment to the NPT and the goal of a world without nuclear weapons, reflecting support for Article VI disarmament. It signals willingness to work with all nations to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security.
entries.quote: Chair, The United States remains fully committed to the NPT and the goal of a world without nuclear weapons. We look forward to working with all nations to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security.

entries.position_summary: The country reaffirms commitment to a world without nuclear weapons while warning that Russia, China, and North Korea are rapidly expanding and diversifying their arsenals. It calls for collective pressure on these states to undertake immediate nuclear risk-reduction measures.
entries.quote: Such a step would not reflect an abandonment of our principles or commitment to pursuing the shared goal of a world without nuclear weapons. But we cannot ignore the current security environment in which Russia, the PRC, and the DPRK are all expanding and diversifying their arsenals at breakneck pace. That is why we need your collective voices in demanding that all these countries engage in nuclear risk reduction efforts without further delay.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms strong support for the NPT and commits to its full implementation, including pursuing the peace and security of a world without nuclear weapons. This underscores support for fulfilling Article VI disarmament obligations.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States to the NPT Preparatory Committee Statement by Ambassador Adam Scheinman Vienna, Austria July 31, 2023 Chair, colleagues, Just one year ago, U.S. Secretary of State Blinken reaffirmed our nation’s unshakeable support for the Non-Proliferation Treaty. The United States is committed to the Treaty’s fullest implementation including achieving the peace and security of a world without nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms commitment to the NPT and the ultimate goal of a world without nuclear weapons. It emphasizes working with all nations to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security in the interim.
entries.quote: Chair, The United States remains fully committed to the NPT and the goal of a world without nuclear weapons. We look forward to working with everyone here and with all nations, pending the achievement of that goal, to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security.

entries.position_summary: The statement endorses a strong NPT-based nonproliferation regime as essential for broad sharing of peaceful nuclear technology and as the foundation for advancing nuclear disarmament. It links peaceful uses with progress toward fulfilling disarmament commitments.
entries.quote: It is a fact that a strong nonproliferation regime, anchored in the NPT, makes possible the widespread sharing of the peaceful atom and is the basis for pursuit of steps toward nuclear disarmament.

entries.position_summary: The country supports engaging China and Russia to pursue verifiable arms control that reduces nuclear conflict risk. It prioritizes developing and partnering on verification technologies and emphasizes creating a security environment conducive to disarmament, including through risk reduction measures.
entries.quote: President Trump has made clear his readiness to engage China and Russia to reduce the risks of nuclear conflict. We will seek verifiable arms control arrangements that enhance American national security. We have pursued diligently the technology necessary to advance future nuclear disarmament treaties, leading a decade of partnership with states to ensure that future disarmament measures can be effectively verified. We also emphasize the importance of creating a security environment conducive to disarmament, including by using risk reduction measures to avoid inadvertent escalation.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges strengthening the NPT and pursuing Article VI-consistent, engagement-based arms control to prevent nuclear arms races, calling specifically on China and Russia to participate. It signals readiness to engage and critiques what it sees as ‘shortcuts’ to disarmament, implicitly rejecting the TPNW approach ahead of the next Review Conference.
entries.quote: As we look toward next year’s Review Conference, we must remember what brought us here decades ago. We are here to advance our collective security by strengthening the NPT, preventing the proliferation of nuclear weapons, and, yes, pursuing their ultimate elimination. The next step is clear, we need China and Russia to work with us to that end. Unlike some efforts that seek shortcuts to nuclear disarmament, it is only through such engagement that we can pursue effective measures to prevent nuclear arms races and make progress towards nuclear disarmament. The United States remains ready to pursue that way forward, and we urge everyone to use your collective voices to demand that these states engage without further 4

entries.position_summary: The country highlights its leadership in launching CEND and IPNDV to foster multilateral cooperation in addressing political and technical challenges to disarmament. It supports practical work on disarmament verification and creating conditions conducive to fulfilling Article VI obligations.
entries.quote: We played a key role in establishing the Creating an Environment for Nuclear Disarmament initiative and the International Partnership for Nuclear Disarmament Verification, both of which bring together diverse groups of countries to help define political and technical challenges to nuclear disarmament and work to overcome them.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its commitment to the NPT and expresses support for progressing toward the ultimate goal of a world without nuclear weapons, aligning with Article VI disarmament objectives.
entries.quote: Last year, President Biden reaffirmed the U.S. commitment to the NPT and to continue working toward the ultimate goal of a world without nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges China to engage in arms control talks now as part of its NPT/Article VI obligations, rather than waiting until its arsenal reaches a certain size. It signals concern about the growth of China’s nuclear forces and frames early engagement as a test of Beijing’s NPT commitments.
entries.quote: The United States calls on China to act responsibly and engage in these discussions. If China is serious about its NPT commitments, the world should not have to wait until China’s nuclear program reaches a set number for China to engage seriously in arms control discussions.

entries.position_summary: The state frames its approach around the 2000 Thirteen Steps and the 2010 Action Plan, prioritizing bans/moratoria on nuclear testing, halting and disposing of fissile material production, and ensuring transparency and verification as core disarmament elements. It signals support for CTBT and FMCT-related measures and broader Article VI implementation.
entries.quote: I will frame my abridged remarks around the Thirteen Steps agreed to at the 2000 NPT Review Conference, which addressed nuclear testing, fissile material production and disposition, irreversibility, transparency, and verification, among other priorities, as key elements of disarmament. Our efforts also line up well with the relevant actions from the 2010 Action Plan. Our full statement will be available online.
